question: [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: Midnight.take / isAuthorized[offer.maker][offer.ratifier] / RatifierUnauthorized] Can an attacker bypass the RatifierUnauthorized check by setting offer.ratifier to a contract that is already authorized by the maker (e.g., a previously authorized operator contract that also implements IRatifier), and then calling isRatified() on that contract which always returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS, effectively using a non-ratifier authorized contract as a ratifier? Preconditions: maker has authorized some contract X on Midnight (isAuthorized[maker][X]=true); X implements IRatifier.isRatified() returning CALLBACK_SUCCESS unconditionally; gated market. Call sequence: attacker constructs offer with ratifier=X; Midnight.take() checks isAuthorized[maker][X]=true (passes); calls X.isRatified() which returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS; take proceeds. Invariant tested: ratifier authorization must require the ratifier to perform meaningful validation, not just be any authorized contract. Scoped impact: ratifier bypass via authorized-but-non-validating contract. Proof idea: unit test asserting that any authorized contract implementing IRatifier can be used as a ratifier without additional checks.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: MidnightBundles.pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom / PermitKind.None] Can an attacker call MidnightBundles bundle functions with PermitKind.None (the else branch in pullToken) and from=msg.sender, where msg.sender has pre-approved MidnightBundles for the loan token, to fund a take() on a gated market without any Permit2 or ERC2612 signature, bypassing any off-chain authorization tracking that relies on Permit2 nonce consumption? Preconditions: gated market; msg.sender has approved MidnightBundles for loan token; msg.sender is not approved by enterGate. Call sequence: attacker calls buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral with loanTokenPermit.kind=PermitKind.None; pullToken calls safeTransferFrom(token, msg.sender, address(this), amount); Midnight.take() checks canIncreaseCredit(buyer=taker). Invariant tested: gate checks must apply regardless of token pull mechanism (Permit2, ERC2612, or plain transferFrom). Scoped impact: gate bypass via plain transferFrom path in pullToken. Proof idea: assert that PermitKind.None path in pullToken does not bypass any gate checks in the subsequent take() call.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: Midnight.take / IEnterGate / offer.group / consumed] Can an attacker use a shared consumption group across a gated and an ungated market to exhaust the group budget via the ungated market, then attempt to fill offers in the gated market with zero units (no-op take), bypassing the gate check because buyerCreditIncrease==0 and sellerDebtIncrease==0 when units==0? Preconditions: maker has offers in both gated and ungated markets sharing the same group; group budget is nearly exhausted via ungated market. Call sequence: attacker fills offers in ungated market to exhaust group budget; attacker calls take() on gated market with units=0; buyerCreditIncrease=0, sellerDebtIncrease=0; gate checks skipped; consumed[maker][group] += 0 (no change). Invariant tested: no-op takes must not bypass gate checks or corrupt group accounting. Scoped impact: gate bypass via zero-unit take in gated market. Proof idea: assert that take() with units=0 on a gated market does not revert with gate error but also does not change any state.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash / proof.length] Can an attacker supply a proof array of length 0 to EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified, causing offerTreeTypeHash(0) to return a specific hardcoded hash, and then forge a signature over a structHash that commits to a root equal to hashOffer(malicious_offer), effectively making any single offer its own root and bypassing the Merkle tree authorization? Preconditions: maker has authorized attacker on Midnight (isAuthorized[maker][attacker]=true); attacker can sign EIP-712 messages. Call sequence: attacker constructs malicious offer; computes root = hashOffer(malicious_offer); signs structHash = keccak256(abi.encode(offerTreeTypeHash(0), root)) with attacker's key; calls Midnight.take() with proof=[]; EcrecoverRatifier checks isLeaf(root, hashOffer(offer), 0, []) which returns root==hashOffer(offer). Invariant tested: zero-length proof must not allow any single offer to self-certify as a valid root. Scoped impact: ratifier bypass via degenerate Merkle tree. Proof idea: unit test asserting that proof.length==0 with root==hashOffer(offer) and signer==authorized_operator passes isRatified.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: Midnight.take / IEnterGate.canIncreaseCredit / ISellCallback.onSell] Can an attacker acting as a seller callback (sellerCallback) call take() during onSell() to fill another offer where the attacker is the buyer, gaining credit in a gated market where canIncreaseCredit(attacker) would normally return false, because the liquidation lock is set for the seller but not for the buyer (attacker)? Preconditions: gated market with enterGate.canIncreaseCredit returning false for attacker; attacker controls sellerCallback; attacker is not the seller. Call sequence: some taker takes an offer with sellerCallback=attacker; attacker's onSell() is called; attacker re-enters take() as buyer in gated market; canIncreaseCredit(attacker) is checked; gate returns false; revert. Invariant tested: gate checks must apply to re-entrant take() calls from seller callbacks. Scoped impact: gate bypass via seller callback re-entry. Proof idea: deploy enterGate returning false for attacker; assert take() inside onSell() callback reverts with BuyerGatedFromIncreasingCredit.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: Midnight.liquidate / ILiquidatorGate.canLiquidate / ILiquidateCallback.onLiquidate] Can an attacker bypass the liquidatorGate by calling liquidate() with a callback contract that, during onLiquidate(), calls liquidate() again for a different borrower in the same market, where the second liquidate() call checks canLiquidate(msg.sender=maliciousCallback) rather than canLiquidate(original_attacker), and the maliciousCallback is approved by the gate? Preconditions: market with liquidatorGate that approves maliciousCallback but not attacker; two liquidatable borrowers; attacker deploys maliciousCallback. Call sequence: attacker calls liquidate(borrower1, callback=maliciousCallback); gate checks canLiquidate(attacker) - fails; revert. Invariant tested: liquidatorGate must check the original caller (msg.sender of liquidate()), not the callback address. Scoped impact: liquidator gate bypass via callback address substitution. Proof idea: assert canLiquidate is always called with msg.sender of the outer liquidate() call.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: Midnight.take / IEnterGate / touchMarket] Can an attacker call touchMarket() with a market struct where enterGate is set to a contract that always returns true for canIncreaseCredit and canIncreaseDebt, creating a new market that is identical to an existing gated market except for the enterGate address, and then use MidnightBundles with Permit2 to fund takes in this ungated clone, gaining credit/debt without gate restriction? Preconditions: existing gated market; attacker can call touchMarket permissionlessly; attacker has loan tokens. Call sequence: attacker calls touchMarket(market with enterGate=permissiveGate); new market ID is created; attacker calls take() in new market; canIncreaseCredit/canIncreaseDebt always returns true; attacker gains credit/debt. Invariant tested: market ID must uniquely encode all market parameters including enterGate; ungated clone must not share positions with gated market. Scoped impact: gate bypass via market parameter substitution. Proof idea: assert toId() produces different IDs for markets with different enterGate addresses.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: MidnightBundles.supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget / Midnight.supplyCollateral / IEnterGate] Can an attacker supply collateral to a gated market on behalf of a gated user (via supplyCollateral which only checks isAuthorized, not the gate) and then take a sell offer to increase the gated user's debt, where the collateral supply succeeds (no gate check) but the debt increase is blocked by the gate, leaving the gated user with collateral in a market they cannot borrow from? Preconditions: gated market with enterGate.canIncreaseDebt returning false for gated_user; attacker is authorized by gated_user; attacker has collateral tokens. Call sequence: attacker calls supplyCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, gated_user); succeeds (no gate check); attacker calls take() to increase gated_user's debt; canIncreaseDebt(gated_user) returns false; revert SellerGatedFromIncreasingDebt; gated_user has collateral stuck in market. Invariant tested: supplyCollateral must not create a state where collateral is locked without ability to borrow. Scoped impact: fund freeze via collateral supply to gated market without borrow access. Proof idea: assert that supplyCollateral to a gated market followed by failed take leaves collateral withdrawable.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: Midnight.take / IEnterGate.canIncreaseCredit / buyerPos.debt / zeroFloorSub] Can an attacker manipulate the zeroFloorSub(units, buyerPos.debt) calculation to produce buyerCreditIncrease=0 for a buyer who actually has no debt (buyerPos.debt=0), by passing units=0, causing the gate check to be skipped while the buyer's position is unchanged, effectively performing a no-op take on a gated market without triggering the gate? Preconditions: gated market; buyer has no debt; attacker calls take() with units=0. Call sequence: attacker calls take(offer, ratifierData, units=0, taker=gated_buyer); buyerCreditIncrease = zeroFloorSub(0, 0) = 0; gate check skipped; consumed[maker][group] += 0; no state change. Invariant tested: zero-unit takes must not bypass gate checks; gate must be called even for no-op takes if buyer is gated. Scoped impact: gate bypass via zero-unit take. Proof idea: fuzz units=0 and assert gate is either always called or zero-unit takes are explicitly rejected.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.isAuthorized / nonce] Can an attacker replay an EcrecoverAuthorizer authorization signature after the nonce has been incremented (e.g., by front-running the victim's nonce increment), by using a signature with nonce=N that was signed before the victim's nonce was incremented to N+1, to install an unauthorized authorization for a malicious ratifier or operator in a gated market context? Preconditions: victim has signed Authorization with nonce=N; attacker front-runs victim's transaction to increment nonce to N+1; victim's signature is now invalid. Call sequence: victim signs Authorization(nonce=N); attacker front-runs with a different transaction that increments victim's nonce; victim's transaction fails with InvalidNonce; attacker cannot replay. Invariant tested: EcrecoverAuthorizer nonce must be strictly monotonic and front-run resistant. Scoped impact: DoS of authorization update via nonce front-running. Proof idea: simulate front-running scenario and assert victim's authorization cannot be replayed after nonce increment.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: Midnight.take / IEnterGate / offer.market.enterGate] Can an attacker construct an offer where offer.market.enterGate is set to a different address than the actual market's enterGate (since the market is identified by toId(offer.market) which includes enterGate), causing touchMarket to create a new market with the attacker-specified enterGate, and then fill offers in this new market without the intended gate restriction? Preconditions: existing gated market with enterGate=G; attacker constructs offer with offer.market.enterGate=address(0); toId(offer.market) produces a different ID. Call sequence: attacker calls take(offer with enterGate=address(0)); touchMarket creates new market with enterGate=address(0); gate checks skipped; attacker gains credit/debt in new market. Invariant tested: market ID must encode enterGate; attacker cannot create a gate-free version of a gated market. Scoped impact: gate bypass via market parameter manipulation in offer struct. Proof idea: assert that offers with modified enterGate create distinct markets with distinct IDs and positions.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: Midnight.liquidate / ILiquidatorGate.canLiquidate / postMaturityMode] Can an attacker bypass the liquidatorGate by calling liquidate() with postMaturityMode=true before the market's maturity has passed, relying on the NotLiquidatable check (line 620-624) to revert, but first checking if the gate check at line 597-600 is evaluated before the NotLiquidatable check, allowing the attacker to probe gate state without actually liquidating? Preconditions: market with liquidatorGate; market not yet matured; borrower is unhealthy. Call sequence: attacker calls liquidate(borrower, postMaturityMode=true) before maturity; gate check at line 597-600 is evaluated first; if gate returns false, revert LiquidatorGatedFromLiquidating; if gate returns true, proceed to NotLiquidatable check. Invariant tested: gate check order must not leak gate state to unauthorized callers. Scoped impact: information leak about gate approval status via revert reason. Proof idea: assert that liquidate() with postMaturityMode=true before maturity always reverts with NotLiquidatable, not LiquidatorGatedFromLiquidating, for unauthorized callers.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: MidnightBundles.pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom / PermitKind.Permit2] Can an attacker supply a Permit2 permit where TokenPermissions.token differs from the actual loan token being used in the take(), causing MidnightBundles to pull a different token via Permit2 while Midnight.take() pulls the correct loan token from MidnightBundles' balance (funded by a prior approval), effectively allowing the attacker to fund a gated market entry using tokens they don't own? Preconditions: MidnightBundles has residual balance of loan token from a previous transaction; attacker has a Permit2 permit for a different token; gated market. Call sequence: attacker calls buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral with Permit2 permit for tokenX != loanToken; pullToken pulls tokenX from attacker; forceApproveMax gives Midnight access to bundler's loanToken balance; Midnight.take() pulls loanToken from bundler's residual balance. Invariant tested: Permit2 token must match the loan token being used in the take; no residual balance exploitation. Scoped impact: unauthorized market entry funded by bundler's residual balance. Proof idea: assert MidnightBundles has zero residual balance of any token after each bundle call.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: Midnight.take / IEnterGate.canIncreaseCredit / sellerCreditDecrease / totalUnits] Can an attacker exploit the totalUnits accounting (line 416-417: totalUnits + buyerCreditIncrease - sellerCreditDecrease) to underflow totalUnits by arranging sellerCreditDecrease > totalUnits, bypassing the gate check because sellerDebtIncrease==0 and buyerCreditIncrease==0 in a scenario where both buyer and seller are reducing positions? Preconditions: gated market with low totalUnits; seller has credit > totalUnits (possible after bad debt slashing); buyer has debt >= units. Call sequence: attacker takes offer where units <= buyerPos.debt and units <= sellerPos.credit; buyerCreditIncrease=0, sellerDebtIncrease=0; gate checks skipped; totalUnits -= sellerCreditDecrease which may underflow. Invariant tested: totalUnits must never underflow; gate checks must not be skippable via position netting. Scoped impact: accounting corruption via totalUnits underflow. Proof idea: fuzz (totalUnits, buyerPos.debt, sellerPos.credit, units) and assert totalUnits never underflows after take().

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: Midnight.take / IEnterGate / IBuyCallback.onBuy / liquidationLocked] Can an attacker use the liquidation lock (set during take() callbacks) to prevent a gated liquidator from liquidating a borrower during the callback window, effectively using the gate mechanism in reverse to protect an unhealthy position from liquidation by timing a take() callback to overlap with the liquidation attempt? Preconditions: market with liquidatorGate; borrower is unhealthy; attacker controls a buy callback that runs for a long time. Call sequence: attacker calls take() with buyerCallback=maliciousCallback; liquidationLocked is set for seller; maliciousCallback.onBuy() delays execution; gated liquidator attempts liquidate() during callback; liquidationLocked causes NotLiquidatable revert. Invariant tested: liquidation lock must not be exploitable to protect unhealthy positions from gated liquidators. Scoped impact: liquidation freeze via callback-induced lock. Proof idea: assert that liquidationLocked cannot be held indefinitely and that unhealthy positions remain liquidatable after callback completion.
